name: Pet Rock
short_details: The ultimate low-maintenance pet: just a rock!
long_details: In 1975, advertising guy Gary Dahl packaged ordinary beach stones with googly eyes, a cardboard box, straw bedding, and a hilarious 'care & training manual'. It sold over 1.5 million units in 6 months, making him a millionaire. Zero food, zero mess, zero effort — pure genius stupidity.
key_feature: Comes with official training manual (sit, stay, roll over... it ignores all commands)
quote: "The pet that never barks, never dies, and never needs walking." — Gary Dahl

name: The Useless Machine
short_details: A box that turns itself OFF when you turn it ON.
long_details: Invented in 1952 by AI pioneer Marvin Minsky at Bell Labs. Flip the switch... a tiny arm pops out, flips it back off, and hides. That's literally the entire function. Commercial kits exploded in popularity in the 1960s and still sell today as the most pointless mechanical device ever built.
quote: "It does absolutely nothing... and does it perfectly." — Every owner ever

name: Banana Phone
short_details: A real banana you hold to your ear to take calls.
long_details: Launched in 2017 via Indiegogo (raised $45k+), appeared on Shark Tank 2022. Bluetooth speaker/handset shaped exactly like a banana — connect to your phone and talk like the 90s cartoon. Average sound, maximum laughs. Still sold worldwide for $30–40.
key_feature: Full Bluetooth + speaker in banana shape
quote: "Ring ring ring ring ring... banana phone!" — The classic song that inspired it

name: Self-Stirring Mug
short_details: A coffee mug that stirs itself automatically.
long_details: Battery-powered (or magnetic) mug with a built-in spinner. Press a button and it whirls your coffee, tea, or hot chocolate like magic. Launched around 2018 and still a top Amazon novelty gadget. For people who find spoons too much work.
key_feature: One-touch automatic magnetic or spinning stirrer
quote: "Why use a spoon when the mug can do the hard work?" — Lazy coffee lovers everywhere

name: Motorized Ice Cream Cone
short_details: A spinning holder that rotates your ice cream for you.
long_details: Battery-powered plastic cone that slowly spins your ice cream so it melts evenly and you never have to twist it yourself. Pure overkill novelty sold on eBay and gadget sites since the early 2010s. Watch it twirl while you eat — the dumbest way to enjoy dessert.
key_feature: Battery-powered slow rotation for even melting
quote: "No more manual turning — let the cone do the work!"

name: Chia Pet
short_details: A ceramic figure that grows green 'hair' from chia seeds.
long_details: Created in 1977 by Joe Pedott after seeing chia seeds in a health store. Over 25 million sold worldwide through legendary 'As Seen on TV' ads. You water it, wait a week, and get a hilarious sprouting head. Still sold today — the original dumb plant pet.
key_feature: Grows real chia sprouts in 7-10 days (just add water)

name: Big Mouth Billy Bass
short_details: A singing mounted fish that moves and talks when you walk by.
long_details: Launched in 1999-2000 by Gemmy Industries. The fish turns its head, opens its mouth, and sings 'Take Me to the River' or 'Don't Worry Be Happy'. Sold millions, became a cultural phenomenon, and is still a gag gift staple.
quote: "Take me to the river..." — Billy himself

name: Shake Weight
short_details: A vibrating dumbbell that 'works' your arms while you hold it.
long_details: Released in 2010 with wildly suggestive infomercials. Sold over 2 million units and made $40 million despite (or because of) the ridiculous workout. Still available — the ultimate lazy fitness joke.
key_feature: Dynamic inertia technology that shakes on its own
quote: "Shake it like you mean it!" — Infomercial voiceover

name: The Clapper
short_details: Clap your hands to turn lights on and off.
long_details: Invented in the 1980s and made famous by 'As Seen on TV'. Clap twice for on, three times for off. Sold millions and became a pop culture icon (even referenced in movies). Still sold today for lazy people everywhere.
key_feature: Sound-activated switch for lamps and appliances

name: Snuggie
short_details: A blanket with sleeves — basically a backwards robe.
long_details: Launched in 2008 with hilarious infomercials showing people doing normal tasks while wrapped up. Sold over 30 million units and spawned copycats. The ultimate lazy-wear fad.
key_feature: Fleece with built-in sleeves for hands-free warmth

name: Banana Slicer
short_details: Plastic tool that slices an entire banana in one perfect motion.
long_details: Became a viral Amazon hit around 2012 with thousands of hilarious reviews. One quick press and you get uniform banana slices. Still one of the most pointless kitchen gadgets ever sold.
key_feature: One-piece plastic slicer for perfect 1-inch slices
quote: "Finally, evenly sliced bananas in seconds!" — Amazon reviewer

name: USB Pet Rock
short_details: A real pet rock that plugs into your USB port.
long_details: ThinkGeek launched this in 2004 as the ultimate joke gadget. It's just a rock with a USB cable — no function, no storage, just vibes. Sold thousands to nerds who loved the absurdity.
key_feature: USB plug for 'charging' your rock (it does nothing)
quote: "The pet rock for the digital age."

name: NoPhone
short_details: A fake phone you hold so you look busy.
long_details: Kickstarter hit in 2014 (raised thousands). It's literally a black plastic rectangle with no screen, no battery, nothing. Sold as an anti-smartphone device for people addicted to their phones. Still available.
key_feature: Exact iPhone size and weight with zero functionality
quote: "The phone for people who hate phones."

name: DVD Rewinder
long_details: Popular novelty gadget in the early 2000s. DVDs don't need rewinding (unlike VHS), but this spinning box did it anyway for laughs. Sold tens of thousands as the ultimate pointless electronic device.
key_feature: Spinning tray that makes fake rewinding sounds
quote: "Because your DVDs deserve the full VHS experience."
